_purpose: These weak/strong calibration pairs are used as exemplars in the LLM prompt so generated answers match the 9/10 caliber bar.

calibrations.question: Tell me about yourself
calibrations.context: Calibration 2 candidate: 5 years PM at Shopify + Series B, applying to Anthropic Applied AI. Stated weaknesses: no direct AI/ML, e-commerce background.

weak.response: So, um, I've been a PM for like 5 years now. I started at a small startup where I did a lot of different things, you know, kind of wore a lot of hats. Then I moved to a bigger company where I worked on growth stuff. I'm really passionate about user experience and I think I'm good at working with engineers.

strong.response:
I've been a PM for 5 years, most recently at Shopify where I led checkout optimization. Last year, 47 experiments, 14% conversion lift, $400M GMV. Biggest win was rebuilding address autocomplete — Google Maps API, predictive suggestions, cut mobile abandonment 8%.

Before Shopify I was at Vettery, built the candidate-matching algorithm from scratch. First real production ML exposure — worked with data science on ranking signals, learned accuracy vs. explainability trade-offs. Still runs today. 50K matches per week.

I want to go deeper on AI, which is why Anthropic. Vettery showed me what ML can do when thoughtfully applied. Shopify taught me rigorous experimentation at scale. Claude needs both. Excited about making AI tools useful for everyday tasks — feels like the next version of conversion optimization.
strengths: 2 minutes, zero hedging
'No-AI' weakness flipped via Vettery
Specific metrics throughout: $400M, 47, 14%, 8%, 50K
Memorable detail: address autocomplete, ranking signals
Clear Anthropic bridge in the closing line

weak.response: My biggest failure was probably when I shipped a feature that didn't get adopted. We built this dashboard and I thought they would love it but they didn't really use it. I think the problem was we didn't do enough user research.

strong.response:
Launching our enterprise tier without proper infrastructure planning. 50-person startup, pushing upmarket. Three Fortune 500s ready to sign for SSO and custom SLAs.

Got the deals. $1.2M ARR. But I hadn't coordinated with engineering on what 99.9% uptime actually required. Within two months, three incidents violated SLAs. $180K in credits. One customer churned.

The mistake was treating the launch like a sales problem when it was a cross-functional operations problem.

What I took from it: big customers require a different operating model, not just a different price point. Next enterprise launch, I created a pre-sales technical review with engineering veto power on SLA commitments. Eight more deals since. Zero violations.

This is why I'm excited about Google — operating at scale needs the cross-functional rigor I learned the hard way.
strengths: Full 2-minute arc; owns the failure confidently
'Small company' flipped to 'learned to operate at scale'
Specifics: $1.2M, 99.9%, $180K, 8 deals
Sophisticated lesson — not PM-101
Google bridge in the closing line

calibrations.question: Tell me about an AI trade-off you made

weak.response: We had to choose between model accuracy and latency. We chose latency because users were leaving. It worked out.
flaws: 'It worked out' is the antithesis of specificity

strong.response:
Search team wanted to switch from BM25 to dense vector retrieval with sentence embeddings. 15% better offline relevance, but 4x infra cost and p99 latency from 80ms → 350ms.

Dug into the data. 70% of queries were simple keyword matches where BM25 was excellent. Dense only mattered for the 30% with semantic ambiguity.

Proposed a hybrid: BM25 for simple queries via a lightweight classifier, dense for complex. 80% of the quality gain at 1.5x cost, p99 under 150ms.

ML team pushed back on maintenance complexity — I acknowledged that and committed to monitoring for misrouted queries.
strengths: BM25, dense vector, sentence embeddings, p99, classifier — used naturally
Third-option thinking, not binary
Maturity on the maintenance trade-off
Specific numbers throughout

calibrations.question: Design a churn-reduction agent for a telecom company
calibrations.context: AI System Design round. The same prompt, two answers — one that fails the round, one that scores 9/10.

weak.response: I'd start with user segmentation. There are new users, power users, at-risk users. Power users drive the most revenue so I'd focus there. I'd build an AI system that predicts churn and reaches out to users. The AI would use their data to decide what to do. For metrics, I'd track retention and engagement.
flaws: No diagram. 'An AI system' is the vague magic box.
No model named. No failure modes. No orchestration. No memory.
No scale discussion. Interviewer writes 'no system thinking' and moves on.

strong.response:
Quick scope: telecom, power users, multi-agent system, 6-month MVP. Sound good?

[Draws diagram]

Interaction layer: mobile app, SMS, outbound call.
Orchestration layer: router that classifies intent and routes between agents, tracks conversation state.
Agent layer: three agents. Data Analyst Agent runs churn prediction with XGBoost — cheaper, faster, more interpretable than an LLM for tabular features, and stakeholders can audit why a user was flagged. Customer Voice Agent uses an LLM because it needs natural language. Executor Agent uses a rules engine + ML recommender — offer logic has to be auditable and business-configurable.
Data layer: call transcripts → sentiment; app usage sessions; billing behavior; outage history. Vector DB for RAG, feature store for ML, policy store for the executor.

Memory: episodic for past customer conversations, session for current state, semantic via RAG for product knowledge.

Does this structure make sense before I go into metrics?

Metrics cascade: recall on churn prediction → escalation rate → retention lift. If recall drops from 90% to 85%, we miss 5% more at-risk users — at our base, ~2,500 accounts per quarter, $1.25M preventable churn.

Failure modes: model down → circuit breaker to human agent. Latency >30s → escalate. Repetitive responses or frustration signals → hand off.

10x scale: bottleneck is the LLM API at ~200ms per call. I'd cache the top 500 query patterns (~40% of volume), on-premise the voice agent for latency, and shard the vector DB by region.
strengths: All five DASME phases present with the right time allocation
Four-layer diagram, three named agents with non-overlapping jobs
XGBoost + LLM + rules — explicitly chosen and justified
Three memory types named
Metric cascade with dollar math
Three failure modes; 10x-scale answer with named bottleneck and three concrete fixes
